**Fictional Monologue – Dr. Marcus Hale**

My name is Dr. Marcus Hale. For thirty-eight years I practiced internal medicine and pediatrics in a mid-sized clinic outside Portland. I gave vaccines. Thousands of them. I believed what I was taught in medical school and what the detail reps from Merck and GlaxoSmithKline told me during lunch rounds: safe, effective, non-negotiable. I repeated the mantra to worried parents the way a priest recites the creed—until the day the data in front of me stopped matching the sermon.

I first paid real attention to VAERS in the late nineties, back when most physicians still treated it like some dusty suggestion box in the basement. The Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System is the only national database we have for post-marketing surveillance of vaccines. It is passive, voluntary, and open to anyone—doctors, nurses, parents, even patients themselves. That openness is usually spun as a weakness. I came to see it as the only honest window we have.

By the time I retired, VAERS contained more than 900,000 reports. Let that number settle for a moment. These are not mild sore arms. We’re talking hospitalizations, permanent disabilities, life-threatening anaphylaxis, encephalopathies, Guillain-Barré, transverse myelitis, seizures that never stopped, and deaths logged with a temporal association to vaccination. The CDC and FDA maintain that the vast majority are coincidental. They like to say VAERS cannot prove causation. Fair enough on its own. But then they treat the same database as reassuring when the numbers look low. That is intellectual sleight of hand.

Here is what they rarely say out loud: a Harvard Pilgrim Health Care study funded by the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality found that fewer than one percent of vaccine adverse events are actually reported to VAERS. Fewer than one percent. The authors themselves called the underreporting “alarming.” The study sat on a government shelf for years before it finally surfaced. When it did, it was quietly ignored by the very agencies that commissioned it.

Do the arithmetic with me. If only one percent are reported, then the true number of adverse events in America alone is not hundreds of thousands—it is tens of millions since the modern schedule began its steep climb. Even if you grant that many of those events would have happened anyway, the scale becomes impossible to dismiss as background noise. When you vaccinate nearly every child in the country on the same rigid timetable, coincidence and causation start to look like the same word wearing different clothes.

I watched colleagues roll their eyes when parents brought printouts from VAERS. “It’s just a database of unverified reports,” they’d say, as though unverified automatically meant untrue. Yet the same physicians had no trouble citing VAERS when a new rotavirus vaccine was pulled from the market in 1999 after only fifteen reports of intussusception. Fifteen verified cases were enough to yank a product off the shelves. But when the count climbs into the hundreds of thousands for other vaccines—autoimmune disorders, neurological regression, sudden infant death—we are told the system is too flawed to trust. The standard changes depending on which conclusion the authorities prefer.

I started pulling the raw data myself. I read the autopsy reports that listed “undetermined” as cause of death two days after a 6-in-1 shot. I spoke with nurses who described infants seizing in their arms minutes after injection, only to have the event re-coded as “fever” or “crib death.” I watched the official narrative harden: vaccines do not cause autism, do not cause SIDS, do not cause autoimmune disease. The studies that supposedly settled these questions were almost all funded by the manufacturers or by agencies whose budgets and prestige rest on public confidence in the schedule. Independent researchers who found signal—Wakefield, Hooker, Mawson, the list is longer than most people realize—were subjected to ritual public destruction while their actual data was left un-replicated or simply disappeared from discussion.

The effectiveness claim fares no better under scrutiny. Measles mortality had already fallen more than 98 percent before the vaccine was introduced. The same pattern holds for scarlet fever, pertussis, and diphtheria—improvements driven by sanitation, nutrition, and antibiotics, not needles. Today we have outbreaks in fully vaccinated populations. The MMR’s protection wanes; that is why we now need “booster” doses that were never part of the original promise. Chickenpox, once a mild childhood rite, was reframed as a serious threat so a lucrative vaccine could be sold. The disease has been replaced by shingles in older adults at rates that should alarm any honest epidemiologist.

I am not a “anti-vaxxer” in the cartoon sense the media paints. I am a physician who spent decades inside the system and finally admitted the data no longer supported the dogma. The public has been told a clean, linear story: vaccines conquered disease, saved millions of lives, and carry risks so vanishingly small they can be ignored. The VAERS numbers, the underreporting studies, the conflicting epidemiological work, the biological plausibility of immune activation gone wrong—all of it suggests that story is marketing, not medicine.

Parents deserve the raw numbers, not sanitized talking points. They deserve to know that a passive surveillance system capturing perhaps one percent of harm is being used to reassure them that everything is fine. They deserve physicians who have read the studies on both sides instead of repeating the slogan “safe and effective” like a liturgical response.

I no longer give vaccines. I no longer tell parents the science is settled. Because it isn’t. The VAERS database, imperfect as it is, keeps ringing the alarm. Sooner or later the rest of us have to stop hitting the snooze button.
